perturbation.original_text: We acknowledge that remedies at law may be inadequate to protect against breach of this Agreement and we hereby agree in advance to the granting of injunctive relief in your favor without proof of actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: We acknowledge that remedies at law may be inadequate to protect against breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the explicit agreement to injunctive relief without proof of actual damages. Pennsylvania law favors protecting trade secrets and confidential information with injunctive relief, but requiring proof of damages provides an additional burden.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets, and the removal of the agreement to injunctive relief in advance without proof of damages may contradict the spirit and application of PUTSA in Pennsylvania by weakening the protections afforded to confidential information.

Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5303 - Injunctive relief Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5303 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5303. Injunctive relief. (a) Injunctions. --Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) Exceptional circumstances. --In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) Affirmative acts compelled by court order. --In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. Please check official sources. This site is pro
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly agreed to injunctive relief without proof of actual damages, while the changed contract removes this agreement. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that Pennsylvania law favors protecting trade secrets with injunctive relief, but removing the agreement increases the burden by requiring proof of damages. The Law Explanation states that removing the agreement may contradict the spirit of PUTSA by weakening the protections afforded to confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 12 PA Cons Stat § 5303 (2022), states that "actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." The original text provides a stronger stance for injunctive relief, while the changed text weakens this protection, creating a legal contradiction by potentially making it more difficult to obtain an injunction.

perturbation.original_text: All copies of the Information, along with our notes on, summaries and compilations of, or excepts from, the Information, except for that portion of the Information which consists of analyses, compilations, forecasts, studies or other documents prepared by us, our agents, representatives or employees, will be returned to you immediately upon your request. That portion of the Information which consists of analyses, compilations, forecasts, studies or other documents prepared by us, our agents, representatives or employees, will be held by us and kept confidential and subject to the terms of this Agreement, or destroyed upon your request, and any oral Information will continue to be subject to the terms of this Agreement. Such destruction will be confirmed in writing to you by us and the person or persons who prepared such documents.
perturbation.changed_text: All copies of the Information, along with our notes on, summaries and compilations of, or excepts from, the Information, except for that portion of the Information which consists of analyses, compilations, forecasts, studies or other documents prepared by us, our agents, representatives or employees, will be returned to you immediately upon your request. That portion of the Information which consists of analyses, compilations, forecasts, studies or other documents prepared by us, our agents, representatives or employees, will be held by us and kept confidential and subject to the terms of this Agreement, or destroyed upon your request, and any oral Information will continue to be subject to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for written confirmation of destruction weakens the verification process and reduces accountability in handling confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Without a written confirmation of destruction, it becomes harder to prove that reasonable efforts were taken to protect the confidentiality of the information, potentially contradicting PUTSA's requirement for maintaining secrecy.

Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The omission of the requirement for written confirmation of destruction directly contradicts the spirit and likely the requirements of maintaining trade secret protection. The Pennsylvania statute snippet defines 'improper means' which includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. Without written confirmation, proving 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, as suggested by the Law Explanation in relation to PUTSA, becomes significantly more difficult. The original text provided a clear mechanism for verification and accountability, which is removed in the changed text, thus weakening the protection of trade secrets and creating a legal contradiction. The absence of written confirmation weakens the evidentiary trail needed to demonstrate compliance with secrecy obligations, increasing the risk of misappropriation and breach of duty.

perturbation.original_text: We shall keep a record of the written Information furnished to us and of the location of such Information.
perturbation.changed_text: We shall endeavor to keep a record of the written Information furnished to us and of the location of such Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall keep" to "shall endeavor to keep" weakens the obligation to maintain a record of the information. This is a significant change that could be detrimental if a dispute over the information arises.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 4009.12
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 4009.12 concerns compelling discovery; a party is generally expected to keep proper documentation and records. Changing "shall keep" to "shall endeavor to keep" creates a loophole and makes it harder for the other party to compel discovery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 231 Pa. Code Rule 4009.12. Answer to Request Upon a Party for Production of Documents and Things. Rule 4009.12. Answer to Request Upon a Party for Production of Documents and Things. (a) The party upon whom the request is served shall within thirty days after the service of the request (1) serve an answer including objections to each numbered paragraph in the request, and (2) produce or make available to the party submitting the request those documents and things described in the request to which there is no objection. (i) Where the documents may be identified only after review of a larger group of documents, and the burden of identifying the documents would be substantially the same for the party serving the request as for the party served, the party served may afford the party serving the request reasonable opportunity to identify the documents, to examine or inspect them and to obtain copies. (b) The answer shall be in the form of a paragraph-by-paragraph response which shall (1) identify all documents or things produced or made available; (2) identify all documents or things not produced or made available because of the objection that they are not within the scope of permissible discovery under Rule 4003.2 through Rule 4003.6 inclusive and Rule 4011(c). Documents or things not produced shall be identified with reasonable particularity together with the basis for non-production; (3) specify a larger group of documents or things from which the documents or things to be produced or made available may be identified as provided by subdivision (a)(2)(i); (4) object to the request on the grounds set forth in Rule 4011(a), (b), and (e) or on the ground that the request does not meet the requirements of Rule 4009.11; (5) state that after reasonable investigation, it has been determined that there are no documents responsive to the request. Official Note The party who is requested to produce documents or things is encouraged to identify the documents or things produced and the documents or things
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall keep" to "shall endeavor to keep" weakens the obligation to maintain records, potentially creating a loophole regarding discovery. Rule 4009.12 outlines the obligation of a party to produce documents and things that are requested, stating "the party upon whom the request is served shall within thirty days after the service of the request (1) serve an answer including objections to each numbered paragraph in the request, and (2) produce or make available to the party submitting the request those documents and things described in the request to which there is no objection." By weakening the initial obligation to "endeavor to keep" records, the modified text makes it easier for a party to argue they do not possess the required records due to a lack of a strict keeping obligation, which contradicts the spirit and intent of Rule 4009.12 which expects a party to maintain and provide records, potentially hindering the discovery process. This falls under Omission Legal Contradiction.
